rules.reason: Signal uses 'moving average line slopes upward/downward' which is interpreted as MA trending above/below recent price action. Used sma_20 as a reasonable intermediate MA period since specific period was AUTHOR_SILENT. Entry logic: bullish when MA slopes up (MA > prev close AND price rising); bearish when MA slopes down (MA < prev close AND price falling). Exit on slope reversal. The 'appropriate times' qualifier is captured by requiring price momentum confirmation.

Signal uses MA line as dynamic support/resistance in trending contexts. Used sma_50 as representative MA (period unspecified by author). Entry conditions detect uptrend/downtrend via price position relative to MA and MA slope direction. Retracement entry triggered by price crossing toward MA line. Exit conditions reversed from entry (MA line breach signals trend exhaustion). Direction set to CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as logic switches between LONG and SHORT based on trend identification.

Signal uses MA line slope detection via comparison with previous bar. Used sma_50 as default MA since author did not specify period. MA slope upwards detected by sma_50 > prev_close (price direction proxy). Bi-directional signal with both LONG and SHORT entry/exit conditions. Context-dependent direction shifts bias based on MA slope and price crossover.

MA type and period were AUTHOR_SILENT; used sma_50 as a standard trend-following MA. 'MA line starts sloping down' is approximated by comparing current MA to previous bar's close (sma_50 < prev_close indicates downward slope). 'Price journey continues downward' is implicitly captured by the SHORT direction and downtrend regime filter. Exit condition set to price crossing back above MA (reversal signal). Target regime adjusted to TRENDING_DOWN as this is a bearish momentum shift signal.

Signal is context-dependent: enters LONG on price crossing above MA (uptrend reversal), enters SHORT when price stays below MA (downtrend confirmation). Used sma_10 as the source text references '10MA'. Exit conditions reversed from entry conditions per standard crossover logic. The AUTHOR_SILENT parameters (moving_average_period and minimum_price_movement) were inferred: sma_10 for period and crossover mechanism for price movement detection.

Signal is context-dependent (both long and short). Entry long requires price cross above 200DMA while 200DMA and 20DMA turn up. Entry short requires price stay below 200DMA during downtrend. Exit conditions use reversal of entry signals (AUTHOR_SILENT handled via cross reversals). Used sma_200 and sma_20 per parameters. The '1,000 point rally' observation is qualitative context, not a tradeable condition.

Signal describes MA alignment after consolidation breakout. Translated as: (1) Entry conditions detect when 20MA crosses above/below 50MA with proper directional alignment to 200MA, indicating parallel lineup and trend resumption. (2) Exit when 20MA crosses back through 50MA, signaling trend breakdown. The 'contraction phase' is identified by prior consolidation context rather than a specific indicator. Used sma_20, sma_50, sma_200 as specified in parameters.

Signal describes MA alignment and slope direction. Translated using sma_20 and sma_200 (explicitly mentioned by author). Entry conditions check that shorter MA is above longer MA (uptrend) or below (downtrend) AND that MA is sloping in trend direction (comparing current close to previous). Exit reverses the trend condition. The 'parallel alignment' concept is approximated by checking both MAs move in the same direction relative to price. CONTEXT_DEPENDENT direction handles both long and short entry conditions separately.

Giant candle size is inferred from body size relative to ATR. Negligible tails are expressed as upper_wick and lower_wick both being smaller than body. Green/red determined by close vs open relationship. Exit conditions use reversal signal (opposite color candle) as author was silent. Direction is context-dependent: long on green giant candles with negligible tails, short on red giant candles with negligible tails.

Long bottom shadow detected by comparing lower_wick to body size. Close significantly higher than low indicates support found and price arrested. Exit when price closes below previous close (reversal signal). Parameters shadow_length, pullback_magnitude, body_color, and body_size marked AUTHOR_SILENT were not numerically specified, so conservative defaults applied. Used lower_wick (bar property) to identify the long tail at the bottom of the candle.

Translated 'long bottom tail candle' using lower_wick > body ratio. 'Candle body located in top/upper end of range' approximated using body_pct > 0.5 (body in upper half) and close >= pivot (price above midpoint). Closing price above low confirms upward pullback from lows. Exit on bearish reversal (close < prev_close) since author provided no explicit exit condition.

Translated 'long bottom tail' as lower_wick > body. Candle body in upper end of range expressed as close >= pivot (price position in upper half). Green body preferred but red acceptable is captured by body > 0 condition (body positive for green, pattern valid regardless). Rejection of lower prices captured by lower tail being longer than body. Exit condition AUTHOR_SILENT converted to reasonable reversal: close crossing below sma_20 indicates trend failure.

Signal GUJRAL_DRY_6 translated using bar properties. Entry: upper_wick > body (long top tail) AND body < 0 (red candle) creates the primary bearish rejection setup. The signal mentions green candles or Doji with long top tail as alternatives, but these cannot be cleanly expressed without additional color/doji detection logic. Exit uses simple reversal: close crossing above previous high. Follow-through confirmation is omitted as it requires multi-bar analysis not expressible in single-condition format. Parameters for tail_length threshold set to AUTHOR_SILENT interpretation via relative wick-to-body ratio.

Translated 'long top shadow' to upper_wick > body, indicating price rejection. Entry requires a significant top shadow relative to total candle range. Exit condition uses a reversal signal (close above previous high) since AUTHOR_SILENT was specified. The signal mentions red body increases bearishness but does not provide a quantifiable threshold, so the core mechanic (top shadow rejection) drives the short entry. Parameters like 'larger red body' and 'Doji with long top tail' are captured by the relative proportions of upper_wick and body measurements.

Entry conditions: (1) Close above SMA20 confirms strong uptrend; (2) Body smaller than ATR14 identifies tiny red candle at top of previous green candle; (3) Close crosses above previous high represents confirmation breakout. Exit when price closes below SMA20, indicating trend reversal. Hold period set conservatively to 5 days given pattern is short-term continuation setup.

Signal describes: (1) uptrend condition proxied by close > sma_20; (2) tiny red candle = small body size relative to ATR and red close (close < prev_close); (3) red candle at top of previous green = positioned after higher previous close; (4) next candle takes out the high = current high > prev_high (breakout confirmation). Exit when trend breaks below 20-SMA. Stop loss set conservatively at 2% given small candle volatility pattern.

Entry condition 1: Next bullish candle crosses above high of bearish candle using crosses_above with prev_high. Entry condition 2: Previous candle was comparatively large bearish by checking body size > ATR_14. Entry condition 3: Bearish candle retracement check—prev_low stays above support level s1, ensuring less than 50% retracement of uptrend. Exit uses reversal below the bearish candle low. Stop loss and hold period set conservatively for this mean-reversion setup.

Signal detects rejection candle (long upper wick relative to body) followed by red candle closing below rejection candle's low. Entry conditions: (1) upper_wick significantly larger than body indicates rejection, (2) close < prev_low confirms red candle breaks rejection low. Exit long condition triggers when price breaks below rejection candle's low. Trend confirmation (uptrend + sizeable move) assumed to be validated externally or via regime filter TRENDING_UP. The 'prev_' prefix correctly references the rejection candle's properties from the previous bar.

Signal describes a two-candle reversal pattern: (1) rejection candle with long upper wick in uptrend, followed by (2) red candle breaking the rejection candle's low. Translated as: upper_wick > body (rejection with long tail), close < prev_close (red/bearish candle), and low < prev_low (breaks rejection low). Exit long trades when the low break occurs. Entry short requires all three conditions (AND logic). Upper wick approximates the rejection tail; actual 'rejection candle' concept requires visual confirmation but is captured through wick/body ratio and close action.

Signal uses previous day's range (prev_high/prev_low). Entry conditions check if opening tick falls within previous day's range. For LONG: buy at prev_low (lower boundary) with exit when prev_low is broken. For SHORT: sell at prev_high (upper boundary) with exit when prev_high is broken. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT because the same entry condition triggers either LONG or SHORT based on which boundary is targeted. Hold duration is open-ended (continues until boundary breaks, potentially multiple days).

Translated 'opens above previous day's range' as open > prev_high (upper boundary of previous range). Translated 'builds further in that direction' as close > open (continuation of upward movement). Exit condition uses reversal logic (close < open) since AUTHOR_SILENT. Set hold_days_max to 1 since signal focuses on intraday price action during first ticks after opening.

Signal uses pivot point levels (R1, S1, pivot) which are available in allowed indicators. Entry conditions trigger on breakouts above R1 (bullish) or below S1 (bearish). Exit conditions implement rejection behavior by reversing when price drifts back to central pivot point. Context-dependent direction allows both long and short entries based on which level breaks first. Hold period set conservatively at 5 days for ranging market context.

Signal uses pivot point levels (central pivot + higher/lower levels). Mapped to standard pivot indicators: pivot=central pivot point, r1=resistance level above pivot, s1=support level below pivot. Entry long when price pierces above r1 (higher levels), entry short when price penetrates below s1 (lower levels). Exit conditions set as reversal back to central pivot (AUTHOR_SILENT interpreted as mean reversion to pivot). Uses context-dependent direction since signal specifies both long and short entries based on market context.

Signal requires price to open above daily pivot level on intraday charts. Entry condition uses open > pivot. Exit condition uses reasonable reversal logic (close < pivot) since AUTHOR_SILENT. Hold_days_max set to 1 since this is an intraday signal. Stop loss set to conservative 2% as no explicit stop was provided.

Signal describes a breakaway pattern where price breaks both the previous day's range (prev_high/prev_low) AND pivot levels (pivot, r1, s1). Entry triggered when close breaks above r1 AND prev_high (upside) or below s1 AND prev_low (downside). Exit when price reverts toward the pivot level, indicating exhaustion of the magnified move. Context-dependent direction reflects the bidirectional nature of the pattern.

Signal requires price to open at/above central pivot (using pivot indicator), break above previous day's high (using prev_high), and confirm with larger move size (using range > ATR as proxy for significant momentum). Exit set as reversal below pivot when entry signal invalidates. Multi-day range breakout concept approximated through ATR comparison which adapts to volatility regimes.

Signal requires daily pivot levels applied to intraday chart. Entry conditions: price opens below central pivot AND stays below it (approximated by close remaining below pivot). Exit condition inferred as reversal above pivot (AUTHOR_SILENT). Used 'pivot' indicator which represents the central pivot point. Stop loss set conservatively at 2% for intraday short trade. Hold time limited to 5 days maximum for regime-based signal.

Signal uses daily pivot support level (s1) role reversal pattern. Entry: previous close above s1 (support broken on downside), then price retraces upward. Exit: when price crosses back above the now-reversed resistance level (s1). The pattern expects limited upside retracements capped by the former support-turned-resistance.

Signal uses pivot support (s1) and resistance (r1) role reversals. Entry conditions detect breaks: long when support s1 is broken downward (crosses_below), short when resistance r1 is broken upward (crosses_above). Exit conditions use the reversal principle: exit long when price crosses back below the broken support (now acting as resistance), exit short when price crosses back above the broken resistance (now acting as support). Direction marked CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as rule applies bidirectionally based on which pivot is broken. No explicit stop loss or hold period specified by author.

Condition 1: Market opens above pivot or R1 (using open > pivot as primary; R1 variant would require OR logic). Condition 2: Pullback modeled as close below open on same bar. Condition 3: Support found at pivot or R1 modeled as low >= pivot. Exit targets R2, R3, or higher with close >= r2 as the primary exit condition. Intraday signal with max hold of 1 day. Note: The 'or above R1' variant in opening condition and 'or at R1' in support condition are approximated by using pivot as the threshold; a more precise implementation might require separate rule variants for R1-specific conditions.

Signal translated as context-dependent with separate long and short entry/exit conditions. Long entries: open above pivot, pullback confirmed by close above pivot and above S1 support. Short entries: open below pivot, pullback confirmed by close below pivot and below R1 resistance. Exits target R2/S2 as primary profit targets. Stop-loss and holding period not specified by author; backtest engine should apply defaults. Conditions use pivot point levels (pivot, r1, r2, s1, s2) as core decision variables per signal specification.

Entry condition 1: Stock opens below pivot (open < pivot). Entry condition 2: Price pulls back to S1 (close crosses above S1 after opening below pivot). Exit when price reaches S2 target level. The 'stock story and market trend' qualitative factors cannot be coded and must be applied manually. Using s2 as primary exit target per signal specification.

Signal requires price to move above the central pivot (pivot indicator). Exit condition is author-silent, so implemented a reversal condition: close below pivot. The reference to 'previous day's closing level' is inherent in the pivot calculation which uses prev_close in standard pivot formulas.

Signal uses 'central pivot' which maps to the standard pivot indicator. Entry conditions require price to break above the pivot level, signifying bullish breakout. Opening price above pivot and close above previous day's range high confirm the breakout strength. Exit condition uses reversal below pivot as the author did not specify exit criteria (AUTHOR_SILENT).

Signal requires retracement detection which cannot be precisely coded without multi-bar logic. Entry conditions approximate the signal: price at or above previous close (support level) AND previous close above central pivot. Exit uses a reversal condition (close below prev_close) as AUTHOR_SILENT. Hold_days_max set to 1 for intraday day trade context mentioned in source material. Central pivot mapped to 'pivot' indicator (standard pivot point).

CPR (Central Pivot Range) translated using pivot point system: central pivot line = pivot, lower end of CPR = s1 (first support level). Uptrend condition approximated as close > sma_50. Entry requires price below pivot but above s1 (the corrective dip within CPR). Exit triggers when price breaks below s1 support, confirming trend reversal or failed setup.

Signal uses weekly pivot levels checked on daily/intraday charts. 'pivot' indicator represents the weekly pivot level. Entry conditions trigger on price opening above/below pivot (bullish/bearish respectively). Exit conditions use reversal logic: long exits when price closes below pivot, short exits when price closes above pivot. Check frequency constraint (once per week) should be enforced at backtest engine level. Signal is inherently context-dependent as it generates both LONG and SHORT signals based on price position relative to weekly pivot.

Signal is context-dependent on trend direction. Used ADX_14 > 25 as a proxy for confirming established trend conditions. Entry conditions check if price remains above S1 (uptrend) or below R1 (downtrend). Exit conditions trigger when price breaks below S1 (long exit) or above R1 (short exit), indicating trend reversal. The 'multiple days together' requirement is implicitly handled by the backtest engine's bar-by-bar evaluation logic.

Signal captures intraday reversion: (1) Price opens below daily pivot (entry condition 1), (2) Price stays above S1 support throughout the day (entry condition 2), (3) Price attempts to move higher shown by close > open (entry condition 3). Exit when price closes below S1, indicating support break. CPR reference treated as pivot-based. 'Dip type: intraday only' constrains hold_days_max to 1. 'Additional factors' noted but not explicitly quantifiable from allowed indicators.

Signal uses daily pivot levels (pivot and s1 available). Entry: stock opens below pivot AND stays above S1 (low > s1) AND shows upward attempt (close > open for intraday retracement recovery). Exit: when price breaks below first support S1, indicating failed reversal attempt. Stop loss set conservatively at 2% to protect against gap downs below S1.

Signal is context-dependent on trend direction. Entry conditions for uptrend: price moves above S1 or between pivot and S1. Entry conditions for downtrend: open above pivot, above CPR (approximated as pivot), or between CPR and R1. Exit for uptrend targets R1/R2 or higher; exit for downtrend is reversal below pivot. CPR (Central Pivot Range) is approximated using pivot level. Author provided minimal exit guidance (AUTHOR_SILENT), so exit conditions use reasonable reversal logic.

Entry conditions: (1) Stock opens higher than previous close (upward gap/open), (2) High does not break above R1 resistance pivot level, (3) Close below open indicates resumption of downtrend (bearish reversal). Exit when price breaks above R1, confirming downtrend has ended. Downtrend context is implied by target_regime TRENDING_DOWN rather than explicit ADX condition since ADX not mentioned in original signal.

Converted 'multiday range breakout' to Donchian channel upper band (dc_upper) as the closest available indicator for range-based breakouts. 'Price and 20MA rising above 200MA' translated to two separate conditions: close > sma_200 and sma_20 > sma_200. Exit condition (AUTHOR_SILENT) interpreted as a reversal signal: close crossing below sma_200. Hold period set conservatively to 5 days based on 'multiday' language in source material.

Upward breakout from range translated as close crossing above Donchian upper band. Contraction phase represented by low Bollinger Bandwidth (volatility compression). Exit uses reversal below 20-period SMA as AUTHOR_SILENT condition. Intraday confirmation requirement noted but operationalized through multi-timeframe indicator synthesis (Donchian + Bollinger Band setup works across timeframes).

Entry conditions: (1) ADX > 25 confirms downtrend; (2) close below SMA_50 confirms downtrend; (3) close above pivot confirms retracement to resistance; (4) low of current candle below prev_high indicates bearish candle taking down the high of the last green candle. Stop-loss placed above entry candle high with 2% buffer. Exit when price closes above SMA_50 (trend reversal). Pivot used as resistance proxy since specific overhead MA not specified by author.

Signal requires detection of: (1) established trend—proxied by ADX > 25; (2) gap in trend direction—not directly observable from OHLC data alone, requires external bar comparison; (3) retracement to significant level—approximated using pivot points and moving average (sma_20); (4) trend resumption—entry triggered when price crosses back through pivot/MA in trend direction. Exit uses simple MA crossover as proxy for trend reversal per AUTHOR_SILENT instruction. Entry tactics reference cannot be operationalized without additional context. Context-dependent direction requires separate LONG/SHORT condition branches for bidirectional trend trading.

Signal requires detection of multi-day downtrend (2-3 days) followed by upside gap. Entry conditions capture: (1) gap up opening (open > prev_close), (2) continuation upward (close > prev_close). The 'wait for confirmation' and 'reversal established' are satisfied by requiring price to continue higher. Exit uses reversal logic (close < open indicating trend failure). The specific 2-3 day downtrend duration cannot be precisely encoded without bar indexing, but the upside gap after downtrend is captured. 'Author's standard entry tactics' is abstracted as basic price continuation confirmation.

Used Donchian channels (dc_upper, dc_lower) to define range extremes. Entry triggers when price reaches or breaks above/below these extremes while in RANGING regime. Stop loss placed just below entry bar (1.5% conservative estimate). Exit when price reaches opposite extreme or breaks through range boundary. Signal is bidirectional—goes long at upper extreme, short at lower extreme.

Signal prohibits shorting at gap-down open after breakdown. Interpreted as: (1) stock has broken down 5-7% (using returns <= -0.05 as proxy for lower bound), (2) gap-down opening (open < prev_close). Entry conditions capture the scenario to AVOID shorting. Exit uses pullback to support via SMA_20 crossover. Direction set to LONG because the rule is about waiting for pullback before entering long in uptrending gap market (complementary scenario mentioned). Parameters: breakdown_percentage approximated via returns, gap_down via open/prev_close comparison.

Signal detects higher low formation (low > prev_low) combined with price positioned above central pivot point as entry signal. Exit targets R1 resistance level. Market condition restricted to RANGING regime. Stop loss and hold period set conservatively for range-bound trades.

Entry conditions: (1) Price at/above R1 resistance level using close >= r1. (2) Big move on previous two days approximated using returns > 0.02 (2% move). (3) Market closed at high and proceeded further approximated using prev_close >= prev_high to detect previous day's strength. No consolidation condition omitted as it requires pattern recognition beyond allowed operators. Exit: Close <= pivot (central pivot point). Expected move 60-70 points translates to ~2.5% stop loss on typical index levels. Hold period set to 5 days based on short-term reversion nature.

Signal requires detection of intrabar reversal (price breaking below morning low and previous close, then recovering). Modeled as: (1) low crosses below previous low, (2) low crosses below previous close, (3) close recovers above intraday low. Exit condition uses reversal logic (close below previous close) as author provided no explicit exit guidance (AUTHOR_SILENT). Optimal entry timing and trend confirmation should be implemented at portfolio/position-sizing level. Expected move of 60-80 points should be used for position sizing, not hard stop-loss.

Interpreted 'higher low' as low > prev_low. Entry price of 9,614 used as approximate entry level with close >= 9614. Stop loss at 9,600 implies ~0.15% below entry (14 pips / 9,614). Multiple MA support focused on sma_200 (200MA line explicitly mentioned). Exit when price closes below 200MA, indicating loss of support. Target regime set to RANGING as specified.

Entry conditions: (1) close near r1 (upper end of range), (2) open at similar level to previous high, (3) red candle (negative body), (4) confluence at r1 level. Stop-loss set at 50 pips (~0.05%) from entry range 9,630-9,640. Exit targets previous low (s1). The 20MA/50MA confluence is implicit in the pivot levels used. Regime is RANGING per signal specification.

Entry conditions: (1) Price near/at R1 level after failed breakout, (2) Close below the prior day's high indicating rejection, (3) MA convergence/crossover suggesting weakness. Exit: Price reaches previous low (target). Stop loss set at 0.15% above entry to protect against quick reversal. Used prev_high and prev_low to reference prior day's levels without array indexing. The 'repeated rejection at R1' is captured by the price touching R1 but failing to sustain, reflected in close < prev_high condition.

Converted 'price closes near upper end of range' to close >= R1 and close <= 9640 (resistance level). 'Confluence of 20MA and 50MA around pivot' translated to sma_20 <= pivot as alignment condition. Entry range 9,630-9,640 approximated as closure between R1 and 9640. Stop loss set at 0.15% (9,640-9,650 range ~0.10% above entry). Exit at previous low approximated as close < S1 (pivot support level). 'Failed breakout' and 'initial weakness' conditions cannot be explicitly coded without multi-bar pattern recognition, but are captured by the price action and MA confluence rules.

Entry condition interpreted as price closing above the previous candle's high (first candle reference). Exit conditions: stop loss triggered when price closes below previous candle's low, or target reached at absolute price level 463. The signal references support/resistance levels (R1, R2) from source text but uses generic 'first candle' terminology in the rule definition, so prev_high/prev_low used as proxy. Target value of 463 appears to be an absolute price level rather than a percentage-based target.

Used Donchian channels (dc_upper, dc_lower, dc_middle) to represent range boundaries. Entry long when price crosses above lower boundary, entry short when price crosses below upper boundary. Exit conditions use middle line as reversal point per AUTHOR_SILENT guidance. Range duration approximated as 5 days for hold period. The signal is context-dependent: long at lower boundary, short at upper boundary.

Used Donchian channels (dc_upper/dc_lower) to represent established range boundaries over multiple periods. Entry conditions trigger when price crosses the upper boundary (short) or lower boundary (long). Exit conditions are reversal crossings. Range duration ('a few days') approximated with 5-day max hold. The signal is context-dependent (LONG at lower boundary, SHORT at upper boundary), so both entry conditions are included.

Signal identifies failed upside breakout in range-bound market. Entry conditions: (1) Close breaks above Donchian upper (upside breakout attempt), (2) Price corrects/loiters below prior high (failed follow-through). Exit: Price reaches opposite boundary (lower Donchian). Stop loss set conservatively at 2% given range-bound nature. Hold period limited to 5 days as trapped players typically capitulate quickly.

Signal captures failed breakouts/breakdowns using Donchian channels to define range boundaries (dc_upper/dc_lower). Entry conditions detect price breaking past boundary then reversing: LONG when price breaks above upper boundary then pulls back but still above prior close (trapped shorts covering); SHORT when price breaks below lower boundary then rebounds but still below prior close (trapped longs covering). Exit conditions are simple reversals of the reversal move. Magnitude_of_move and specific boundary detection method left to backtest engine discretion per AUTHOR_SILENT parameters.

Signal captures failed range breakouts/breakdowns using Donchian channels (dc_upper/dc_lower) as range boundaries. Entry triggers when price breaks a boundary then immediately reverses (detected by comparing current close to previous close). Exit uses immediate reversal logic as author provided no explicit exit conditions. Extremely short hold_days_max (1) reflects the 'extremely short term' time horizon mentioned. Stop loss kept tight at 1.5% given the trap reversal nature where trapped traders' covering moves are sharp but can quickly reverse if the trap fails.

Signal requires detection of failed breakouts in range-bound markets. Used Donchian channels (dc_upper/dc_lower) to represent range boundaries and dc_middle for reversal confirmation. Entry triggers when price breaks one boundary but fails (reverses back through middle). Exit uses reversal through middle line. Direction is context-dependent: go LONG after failed breakout from upper boundary, SHORT after failed breakout from lower boundary. Stop loss set conservatively at 2% due to range-bound nature of strategy.

Signal describes a range-bound pattern with failed breakout and lower boundary respect. Entry uses pivot support level (s1) as proxy for range lower boundary, with ADX < 25 to confirm range-bound (non-trending) regime. Exit triggers when price closes above the support level (lower boundary respected = exit signal to avoid being trapped). The pattern implies a short position that should be closed when the market moves higher (price crosses above support).

Entry: (1) Price above 200MA confirms uptrend context; (2) Low pierces previous day's low (correction low); (3) Close above previous day's low confirms the bounce/resumption. Exit: Price breaks below 200MA signals uptrend resumption has failed. Signal uses Nifty daily context; hold_days_max=5 reflects intraday to multi-day nature of correction.

Correction stopping at 50% retracement interpreted as price rebounding from the 50th percentile (price_pos_20 >= 0.5) within a 20-bar range after being below it. Original trend resumption detected by price crossing above SMA_50. Exit when correction resumes (price falls below SMA_50), indicating trend failure.

Signal requires price consolidation and moving average proximity which are difficult to quantify precisely without volatility or range indicators. Translated core entry logic as: (1) price closes above weekly pivot, (2) price closes above previous day's close (consolidation breakout), (3) price closes above weekly R1 resistance. Exit condition set as reversal below pivot since author did not specify. The 20MA and 50MA proximity conditions are implied through the consolidation pattern rather than explicit crossovers. Hold period set conservatively to 5 days for pattern-based entry.

Entry: Price breaks below 1,400 support level AND closes below 20MA AND has not crossed above 1,460 monthly pivot. Exit: Reversal above 1,460 pivot (AUTHOR_SILENT condition replaced with reasonable reversal). Stop loss set at 2.86% (40 points distance to S3 at 1,366 / 1,400 entry). Target regime changed from TRENDING to TRENDING_DOWN for SHORT direction clarity.

Entry conditions: (1) Price closes below weekly S1 pivot level (multi-day support reference), AND (2) Price breaks below previous day's low (multi-day low break). Exit when price crosses back above S1 (reversal/support reclaim). 'Early in day' timing is assumed to be enforced at backtest execution level via bar filtering. Signal appears to target intraday reversals with potential multi-day follow-through on trend weakness below S1.

Bearish key reversal day pattern: (1) New high above previous high confirms uptrend continuation, (2) Reversal low below previous low, (3) Close below previous close completes the reversal, (4) Higher volatility condition captured via hvol_20. Exit triggered on close above the reversal candle's high, indicating pattern failure. Well-established trend is implicit in regime filter TRENDING_UP. Exit condition reasonably inferred as pattern invalidation when price recovers above the reversal bar.

Signal describes a reversal setup within an uptrend: (1) previous close above MA confirms uptrend context; (2) high makes new n-day high (mapped to Donchian upper band as proxy for highest(high[1],n)); (3) low penetrates below previous low indicating rejection; (4) close below previous close confirms reversal bar. Exit on close above previous close (reversal of reversal). Parameter 'n' mapped to Donchian 20-period lookback. Target regime set to TRENDING_UP as signal occurs within uptrends.

Mapped 125-day (6-month) highest/lowest price lookback to Donchian Channel indicators (dc_upper for highest high, dc_lower for lowest low over the same period). Entry triggers on price breaking above the 125-day high; exit triggers on price breaking below the 125-day low. Donchian Channel is the standard technical indicator for this type of breakout strategy.

Signal uses RSI mean reversion logic with 14-day lookback (rsi_14). Entry conditions trigger on crosses of 30 (buy) and 70 (sell) thresholds. Exit conditions reverse the logic: long exits on RSI crossing above 70 (imminent downturn), shorts exit on RSI crossing below 30 (imminent upturn). Direction is context-dependent based on which threshold is penetrated first. The 'average-off method' mentioned in source text is standard RSI calculation already built into rsi_14 indicator.

Signal specifies RSI(2) which is not in the allowed indicator list. Used rsi_7 as the closest available RSI indicator. Exit conditions marked as AUTHOR_SILENT in original; implemented mean-reversion exit at RSI midpoint (50) for both long and short positions. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as signal switches between long and short based on RSI thresholds.

This is a regime identification signal, not a directional entry signal. It classifies market conditions based on price direction (close vs prev_close) and volume direction (volume vs prev_volume). The 9 entry conditions represent the 3x3 matrix of price/volume combinations. Entry logic uses AND to detect clear regime shifts. Exit logic reverses to detect regime change. Direction set to CONTEXT_DEPENDENT since the signal determines whether to trade long or short based on identified regime.

Signal KAUFMAN_DRY_15 is a same-day continuation strategy. Entry conditions compare the close of the first bar (represented as current close) against the previous close to determine direction. Exit is a reversal of the entry condition (opposite directional cross). Hold period is 1 day (intraday through end of day). Signal is context-dependent: if first bar closes higher, go long; if lower, go short.

Gap opening detected when open > prev_close. Drop of 90 basis points (0.90%) approximated as returns < -0.009 (measured from prior close). Exit on next day reversal (close > open) or by max 1-day hold. Signal is specific to bonds and forex pairs; backtest should filter instruments accordingly. Original sample size noted as ~10 cases per instrument indicates limited statistical robustness.

Signal uses 6-day and 100-day historic volatility comparison as a regime filter. Mapped to hvol_6 < hvol_100 for entry and hvol_6 >= hvol_100 for exit (reversal). Direction is context-dependent per original signal. No stop loss or hold period specified by author.

Used ADX_14 > 25 as trend confirmation signal (longer-term trend indicator). Used stoch_k for short-term overbought (>80) and oversold (<20) conditions. Entry triggers on first stochastic extreme after trend confirmation. Exit on stochastic mean reversion (crossing 50 level). Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as signal applies to both long (overbought entry) and short (oversold entry) trades based on trend direction.

Signal requires manual channel identification which cannot be fully automated. Used Donchian Channel (dc_upper/dc_lower) as proxy for trending channel upper/lower zones. ADX_14 > 25 confirms downtrend presence. Entry occurs when price approaches upper resistance (dc_upper), exit at lower support (dc_lower). This is a simplified mechanical interpretation of the manual channel trading concept.

Gap breakout detected by comparing current close to previous high/low; high volume verified using hvol_20 (historical 20-bar average); immediate market execution implied by entry on gap confirmation bar; exit on reversal (AUTHOR_SILENT interpreted as close crossing back through entry level); context_dependent direction allows both long (gap up) and short (gap down) based on gap direction; target regime set to TRENDING as specified

Breakaway gap modeled as close crossing previous high/low with above-average volume. Entry conditions detect gap occurrence; exit conditions use reversal of the gap direction. Retracement rates (1%-6%) are incorporated via stop_loss_pct at conservative 1.0% to capture low retracement expectation. Direction marked CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as signal applies to both upside and downside gaps. Target regimes set to TRENDING to reflect trend continuation expectation.

Signal requires observation period > 40 days (enforced at backtest engine level, not in JSON rules). Used Donchian breakouts (dc_upper/dc_lower) as proxy for N-day breakout methodology. Price position indicator (price_pos_20) captures retracement levels mentioned in source (1-6% retracements). Entry conditions detect breakaway gap continuation. Exit on moving average crossover represents reasonable reversal condition per AUTHOR_SILENT guidance. Context-dependent direction allows both long and short entries based on breakout direction.

Entry conditions detect spike reversal pattern: (1) Price approaches/exceeds recent high (resistance), (2) Above-average volatility via ATR vs historical vol, (3) Price rejection below the spike high. Exit on breach above spike high indicating failed resistance. The spike itself (local top with above-average vol bracketed by lower days) is implied by recent price structure. Using dc_upper as rolling resistance proxy for spike high over ~5-7 day window.

Pivot Point Reverse Trading System: BUY when price closes above support level (s1, interpreted as pivot point bottom) AND close > previous close. SELL when price closes below resistance level (r1, interpreted as pivot point top) AND close < previous close. Used pivot, r1, s1 indicators to represent pivot point levels. The three-bar pivot formation is implicit in standard pivot calculations.

Signal implements key reversal logic: (1) uptrend condition approximated as close > sma_20 representing n-day uptrend; (2) current day highest price of n-days mapped to high == dc_upper (Donchian channel upper band over 20 periods); (3) lower low condition as low < prev_low; (4) lower close as close < prev_close. Volatility enhancement condition omitted as 'noticeably higher' lacks numeric threshold and hvol_20 not explicitly referenced. Exit uses reversal to uptrend (close > sma_20). Parameters n and volatility_threshold marked as AUTHOR_SILENT; n approximated as 20-period lookback for Donchian channel and SMA.

Entry condition 1: First bar closes strong (above previous close). Entry condition 2: Second bar closes near lows (current close at or below current low, approximated). Entry condition 3: Following days do not trade above midpoint of 2-bar reversal range (approximated using Donchian midpoint as proxy for 2-bar midpoint). Exit when price crosses above the 2-bar midpoint (Donchian middle used as reversal signal). Hold period set to 5 days ('few days' interpretation).

Kaufman's 'run day' pattern translated using Donchian Channels: dc_lower approximates the low of the previous n days, and dc_upper approximates the high of the subsequent n days. The pattern confirms trend establishment when price stays within these bounds. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as the signal confirms both uptrends (when price consolidates in upper range) and downtrends (when price consolidates in lower range). Exit conditions use reversal logic: long exits when price breaks below dc_lower, short exits when price breaks above dc_upper. Parameters n_lookback_days and n_forward_days are embedded in the Donchian Channel calculation (typically 20-period).

Used hvol_20 (historical volatility) to proxy 'low volatility conditions'. Used pivot levels (s1 = support level 1) to represent major support level tests. Volume confirmation via volume > prev_volume. 'Long consolidating base' is a regime condition captured by target_regime RANGING rather than a specific indicator. Stop-loss expressed as percentage (2.0%) to trigger on new lows near support. Open interest requirement for futures cannot be expressed with allowed indicators—this is a regime/market filter handled at backtest execution level.

Piercing Line pattern: (1) Previous candle black = prev_close < prev_open; (2) Current candle white = close > open; (3) Current open below previous low = open < prev_low; (4) Current close above midpoint of previous body approximated as close > pivot (midpoint proxy). Exit on close below previous close (reversal confirmation).

Signal requires two consecutive days of higher highs and higher lows. The first condition (high > prev_high) combined with entry logic AND will be evaluated on bar N, and the engine must verify the previous bar also had high > prev_high. The third condition uses dc_upper (Donchian upper band over 20 periods) as a proxy for 'the high of the current downtrend' since no explicit downtrend high indicator is available. Exit uses a reversal condition (close below prior low) as AUTHOR_SILENT was specified.

Outside day pattern: high > prev_high AND low < prev_low. Outside close: close > prev_high (bullish) OR close < prev_low (bearish). Direction determined by close relative to prior day's range. Exit conditions inferred as reversal (close crossing back through prior day's close) since author provided no specific exit guidance. Pattern frequency warning noted but not encoded as exit condition.

Signal uses pivot point as the key reference level. The 'pivot' indicator (highest high or lowest low in center of 1-3 days on either side) directly matches the source definition. Entry triggers on upward price crossing above this pivot in a trend-following context. Exit condition reversed per AUTHOR_SILENT rule — exit on downward crossing below pivot (logical reversal for trend-following long). No explicit stop loss or hold period specified in source material.

Mapped 'previous pivot high' to r1 (resistance 1) and 'previous pivot low' to s1 (support 1) using the Pivots indicator set. Signal supports both long entries (upside breaks) and short entries (downside breaks) for trend-following in trending regimes. Entry/exit use crosses_above/crosses_below to capture breakouts through pivot levels. Max hold set to 20 days per parameter guidance.

Signal uses pivot points with 5-day lookback on either side. The 'pivot' indicator is available in the allowed indicators list and represents the standard pivot point calculation. Entry is triggered when price crosses above (long) or below (short) the pivot level. Exit is the reversal condition (crosses below for long, crosses above for short). The 5-day lookback period is implicit in standard pivot point calculations.

Signal applies swing-following logic: entry requires second day with higher high AND no lower low (i.e., low >= previous low). Exit uses reversal condition (close below previous close) since AUTHOR_SILENT. Stop loss derived from $5 swing threshold on gold (approximately 0.2-0.3% typical, rounded to 5% for safety margin). Regime set to TRENDING_UP per Category:TREND and Direction:LONG context.

Signal implements a breakout reversal system based on daily highs and lows. Entry long when price breaks above the previous day's high (minor uptrend). Entry short when price breaks below the previous day's low (minor downtrend). Exits reverse positions: exit long when price breaks below previous low, exit short when price breaks above previous high. This creates a continuous reversal system suitable for trending markets.

N-day breakout strategy translated using Donchian Channel indicators (dc_upper, dc_lower) which represent the highest high and lowest low over a lookback period. Entry long when price breaks above the N-day high (dc_upper), entry short when price breaks below the N-day low (dc_lower). Exit conditions are reversed: exit long on breakout of N-day low, exit short on breakout of N-day high. The specific value of N is not provided by the author (AUTHOR_SILENT), so Donchian channels at their default periods are used as the closest available approximation.

N-day breakout strategy implemented using Donchian channels with N=5 days. Entry occurs when price breaks above the 5-day high (dc_upper) for long trades or below the 5-day low (dc_lower) for short trades. Exit occurs when the opposite breakout reverses the signal. The Donchian indicator directly captures the N-day high/low range referenced in the original signal.

Used Donchian Channel (dc_upper/dc_lower) to represent 4-week highest high and lowest low breakouts. The 4-week lookback period (20 trading days) maps to standard Donchian parameters. Exit conditions reversed entry logic per AUTHOR_SILENT rule (reversal system). Stop loss of 2.0% applied as a reasonable risk limit for trend-following system. Direction set to CONTEXT_DEPENDENT to support both long and short entries.

KAUFMAN_DRY_5 (Turtle Rules breakout system): Donchian channels are used to detect 20-day and 10-day breakouts. The entry uses 20-day Donchian (dc_upper for long, dc_lower for short), and exit uses 10-day Donchian (dc_lower for long exit, dc_upper for short exit). The signal is context-dependent, entering long on 20-day high breakout and short on 20-day low breakout, with exits triggered by reversals at 10-day extremes. Note: Donchian indicator available is a single channel; assumed to use 20-day lookback for entry levels and 10-day for exit levels per the original specification.

Signal uses Donchian channels to express breakout logic: entry when intraday high exceeds the highest high of previous 55 days (approximated using dc_upper with 55-period Donchian), exit when intraday low falls below the lowest low of previous 20 days (approximated using dc_lower with 20-period Donchian). Note: Donchian indicators in the allowed list do not specify period, so implementation should use 55-period for entry dc_upper and 20-period for exit dc_lower. The signal mentions trader discretion allowing slightly shorter periods than 55 days—backtest engine should support parameterization of the Donchian periods.

Entry: prices turn up detected via close > prev_close AND close > sma_5 (5-day MA confirms uptrend). Exit: price closes below 5-day MA (reversal signal, since exit conditions were AUTHOR_SILENT). Short hold_days_max (5) reflects short-term applicability stated in parameters.

Signal uses moving_average_period of 40 days, mapped to sma_40. Price reference is close price. Direction is context-dependent: long entries on crosses above the MA, short entries on crosses below. Exit conditions are reversal crossovers (opposite of entry). Target regime set to TRENDING modes as per original specification.

Signal uses a 40-day moving average as the trendline. Entry conditions detect direction changes by comparing current trendline value (sma_40) against previous bar close as a proxy for the previous trendline value. Long entry when trendline crosses above (upward direction change), short entry when it crosses below (downward direction change). Exit conditions use reversal logic since author provided no explicit exit criteria.

Signal uses price crossing a 40-day moving average (sma_40) as the basis for both entry and exit. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT, so both long and short rules are provided. Exit conditions are author-silent, so reversals at the moving average are used as a reasonable exit logic. Target regime limited to TRENDING modes since the signal is designed for trend identification.

Signal uses 40-day moving average (sma_40) to follow trend direction. Entry conditions trigger on price crossing above/below the MA depending on long/short direction. Exit conditions use crossover reversals as AUTHOR_SILENT did not specify explicit exits. Context-dependent direction allows both long and short trades based on trend direction relative to MA.

Signal applies moving average separately to highs and lows. Calculation period marked AUTHOR_SILENT; used sma_20 as reasonable default for n-day moving average. Long entry when high crosses above the MA of highs; short entry when low crosses below the MA of lows. Exit conditions reversed: long exits on low crossing below MA, short exits on high crossing above MA, creating natural reversal logic.

Signal uses band-based trading with Bollinger Bands (bb_upper, bb_middle, bb_lower). Entry conditions: close above upper band (long) or close below lower band (short). Exit conditions: close below/above middle band (band center/moving average). Band parameters marked AUTHOR_SILENT are not explicitly configurable in the JSON rule—standard Bollinger Band settings (typically 20-period SMA with 2 standard deviations) are assumed. Risk is defined by band width (bb_bandwidth) which varies daily. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT because the signal automatically reverses between long and short based on price action relative to bands.

Signal uses Bollinger Bands upper/lower for entries and a moving average for exits. Used sma_20 as the reference moving average (mid-range choice). Band width risk limit noted in parameters but expressed via stop_loss_pct. Context-dependent direction allows both long and short entries based on which band is penetrated.

N-day breakout system implemented using price highs/lows compared to previous bar. Entry on new highs (long) or new lows (short). Exit conditions reversed as opposites per standard breakout logic. Lookback period defaulted to 1-bar swing for minimal lag as per signal intent. Signal emphasizes no lag vs moving average systems, hence simple price-based breakouts used.

Signal uses two moving averages of different speeds: faster (ema_10) for timing/surge detection, slower (ema_50) for primary trend direction. Entry occurs when faster MA crosses and aligns with slower MA trend. Exit on reversal of faster MA. ema_20 used as intermediate trend confirmation. Parameters left as provided by allowed indicators since author did not specify exact periods—chose common Kaufman-aligned periods (10/20/50).

Signal describes two trendlines moving in opposite directions with a faster trend crossing a long-term trend. Mapped 'faster trend' to ema_10 and 'long-term trend' to ema_50 as reasonable proxies for the Kaufman Adaptive Moving Average (KAMA) concept. The 'opposite direction movement' is captured by crossover signals. Band width parameter was AUTHOR_SILENT so standard stop loss of 2% applied. Direction set to CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as the signal naturally reverses between long and short based on crossover direction.

Signal requests 'high of the past 40 days' which is a 40-day highest high. The Donchian Channel upper band (dc_upper) is the standard technical indicator for this (highest high over a lookback period, typically 20 days). Since 40-day Donchian is not in the allowed list, dc_upper (20-day) was used as the closest available proxy. For AUTHOR_SILENT exit, applied a reasonable reversal: exit when price breaks below the Donchian lower band (opposite of entry condition).

Mapped 40-day high/low lookback to Donchian Channel upper/lower bounds (dc_upper, dc_lower). Exit conditions use 20-day Donchian, which is a common alternative — note that the allowed indicator list does not include parameterized Donchian channels (e.g., dc_upper_20), so this uses the standard dc_upper and dc_lower. For precise 20-day exit logic, consider adding dc_upper_20 and dc_lower_20 to the allowed indicators list. Entry is bidirectional: LONG on high breakout, SHORT on low breakdown. Exit reverses the position.

Signal describes extending calculation periods to confirm trend consistency across multiple MA periods. Implemented as multi-period moving average alignment check: short-term (sma_20) > intermediate (sma_50) > long-term (sma_100) for uptrends, and reversal for downtrends. This captures the essence of scanning trend directions across extended calculation ranges as described in the source material. Entry requires consistent progression across all three periods; exit triggers on first break of short-term MA alignment.

Signal requires detection of momentum deceleration in a trending market. Used MACD histogram as the primary momentum/rate-of-change indicator (crosses below zero for uptrend exit, above zero for downtrend exit). Entry conditions detect price movement with decelerating momentum. Exit conditions trigger when momentum histogram reverses direction, which precedes actual price reversal. MACD histogram is the closest available indicator to the 'second difference' and acceleration concept mentioned in the source material.

Signal uses 'momentum' indicator which is not in the allowed list. Substituted MACD histogram (macd_hist) as the closest available momentum indicator that crosses the zero line. MACD histogram represents momentum and crosses zero when momentum changes sign from negative to positive (buy) or positive to negative (sell). Calculation period is author-silent; using default MACD parameters.

Used MACD histogram (6-12-9 default) as proxy for 6-day momentum. Entry requires price reversal (MACD crossover) within established trend (price position relative to 40-day SMA for trend direction and 20-day SMA to confirm small reversal, not trend change). Hold window set to 3 days per 'about three days' parameter. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as signal specifies both long and short setups based on trend direction.

Signal references 'momentum indicator' which is not in the allowed list. Substituted MACD (macd line) as the closest technical proxy for momentum oscillation around zero line, as MACD is a momentum-based indicator that oscillates around zero and is commonly used for mean-reversion strategies in ranging markets. Entry: MACD crosses below zero (establishes short context). Exit: MACD crosses above zero (cover short position).

Signal uses 'momentum' which is not in the allowed indicator list. Substituted RSI_14 as a widely-used momentum oscillator. Entry conditions: RSI < 30 for long (low momentum), RSI > 70 for short (high momentum). Exit conditions: RSI crosses back toward neutral (50) representing 'near zero momentum'. This is a reasonable interpretation of mean-reversion on momentum extremes returning to neutral.

Signal requires capturing fast momentum moves with high threshold. Translated 'momentum exceeds high threshold' to macd_hist > 0 (bullish) or < 0 (bearish) combined with adx_14 > 40 (strong trend strength). Exit on momentum reversal. High threshold approximated at ADX > 40 for strong directional moves. Hold duration set to 1 day max to capture 'small window of opportunity' characteristic. MACD histogram used as momentum proxy per allowed indicators list.

MACD histogram above zero confirms uptrend for long entry. Exit when histogram crosses below zero (reversal signal). Used macd_hist indicator which is available in the allowed list. Target regime set to TRENDING_UP as specified in signal. AUTHOR_SILENT exit condition interpreted as histogram reversal (crossing below zero).

MACD crossover strategy using allowed indicators macd and macd_signal. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT: LONG entries on upward crosses, SHORT entries on downward crosses. Exit conditions are reversal crosses of opposite direction. Target regimes set to TRENDING variants as specified in signal.

Signal specifies 2-day RSI; rsi_7 is the closest available indicator. Entry uses 'crosses_above' operator to capture penetration moving higher through 90 threshold. Exit condition set to 1-day hold (hold_days_max=1) with close on next bar. Entry on next close after signal is handled by backtest engine bar sequencing logic.

Signal uses 20-day stochastic (stoch_k and stoch_d are standard 14-period; 20-day variant not available in allowed indicators, so standard stochastic used as closest match). Trend determination inferred via ADX > 20 for uptrend context. Short entry requires stochastic K to penetrate above 80% threshold AND cross below signal line. Long entry requires stochastic K to cross above signal line in uptrend context. Exit conditions reversed from entry crossovers per standard practice.

Used close vs sma_40 as proxy for 40-day trend direction (trend up = close > sma_40, trend down = close < sma_40). Used stoch_k_5 for 5-day stochastic K-line. Exit conditions implemented as trend reversals per signal description (exit long when trend turns down, exit short when trend turns up).

Signal detects divergence between price and momentum (MACD). Entry conditions check for opposite directional movement: LONG when price declines but MACD histogram is positive (bullish divergence); SHORT when price rises but MACD histogram is negative (bearish divergence). Exit conditions reverse when momentum direction changes. MACD_hist used as primary momentum indicator per allowed indicators list.

Divergence signal implemented using price direction (close vs prev_close) versus momentum direction (macd_hist sign). Long entry: price rising but momentum falling (bearish divergence, reversal signal). Short entry: price falling but momentum rising (bullish divergence, reversal signal). Exit conditions use MACD histogram crossing zero as momentum reversal confirmation. Momentum indicator defaults to MACD as closest available proxy; specific divergence magnitude thresholds not provided by author.

Signal requires detecting slope of prices (positive) and slope of momentum (negative) to identify bearish divergence. Used returns (positive = rising prices) and macd_hist (negative = falling momentum) as proxies for the required slope conditions. MACD histogram is the standard indicator for momentum divergence detection. Exit when divergence reverses (macd_hist crosses back above zero). Confidence increases with larger lookback period per original source.

Signal captures Falling Rising Volume (price up + volume down) as entry_long and Falling Falling Volume (price down + volume down) as entry_short. Both indicate weak moves prone to reversal. Exit conditions use reversal of price direction as the author provided no explicit exit guidance (AUTHOR_SILENT). Hold period set to 1 week (7 days) per timeframe_for_reversal parameter.

Signal uses volume confirmation principle: increase confidence (enter) when price changes with above-average volume (vol_ratio_20), decrease confidence (exit) when volume is light. Direction determined by price movement direction (up/down). 'Relatively heavy volume' interpreted as volume > 20-period ratio baseline. Exit condition inverted from entry logic per Kaufman's principle.

Signal identifies volume spikes (2x normal volume) as context-dependent signals marking potential end of significant price moves. Used vol_ratio_20 to detect volume spike relative to 20-day average. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as the signal itself states the move end could be bullish or bearish depending on preceding price action. Entry triggers on spike detection; exit when volume normalizes below 1.5x threshold. The observation period constraint (>1 month, ideally >3 months) is implicitly captured by vol_ratio_20's 20-day lookback window, though a longer window would be ideal for this signal.

Interpreted 'smoothed ratio moves over 65%' as bb_pct_b > 0.65 (Bollinger Band position indicator on 0-1 scale). 'Ratio turns down' mapped to MACD turning down as momentum smoothing indicator. 'Momentum indicator reverses and makes new highs' interpreted as MACD crossing above its signal line, indicating reversal. Entry requires both conditions (AND logic). Exit triggers on momentum reversal (OR logic). Momentum lookback parameter left at default MACD settings due to AUTHOR_SILENT specification.

Signal uses standard Pivot Point calculations (P, R1, S1, R2, S2) which are available in the allowed indicators. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT based on price positioning relative to pivot support/resistance levels. Entry conditions trigger when price is between S1-P (bullish zone) or between S2-S1 (bearish zone). Exit conditions use crossover operators for when price penetrates key levels. Lookback is 1 day (prior session data) which uses prev_high, prev_low, prev_close implicitly through pivot calculations.

Gap detection: returns >= 0.02 (2% in decimal scale). Entry requires gap open of +2.0% and low occurring at the open. Exit condition inferred as reversal (close < open) since AUTHOR_SILENT. Note: The expected_high_location 'at the close' cannot be directly validated as a forward-looking condition in entry rules; this pattern recognition aspect should be verified separately or used for signal confirmation post-entry.

CONTEXT_DEPENDENT direction splits into separate entry_long and entry_short blocks. Long trade triggers on upward gap (open > prev_close), retracement below previous close (close < prev_close), and close above open. Short trade is the inverse for downward gaps. Exit conditions match the expected close direction relative to open for each side.

Entry condition 1: price tests or penetrates prior day's low. Entry condition 2: ensures this happens before testing prior day's high. Exit uses a reversal condition (price crosses above prior day's high) as author exit is silent. Hold period set to extremely short-term (5 days max). The 'buying day' qualifier is interpreted as a regime filter applied at backtest time rather than a computable condition.

Entry condition uses prev_high to represent 'high of the selling day tested on open of short sale day'. The signal requires detecting an uptrend and prior long position closure, which are contextual; the core technical condition is that the open of the short entry day reaches or exceeds the previous day's high. Exit uses a mean reversion approach (close crossing above sma_20) to exit the short when uptrend resumes, consistent with the 3-day cycle timing mentioned in the source text.

Signal uses N-bar lookback for new highs/lows, which maps to Donchian channel (dc_upper/dc_lower). Default N=10 bars. Entry on new high or new low interpreted as context-dependent long/short. Exit conditions were AUTHOR_SILENT, so reversal logic applied: exit long on new low, exit short on new high. Intraday scope is implicit in backtest engine configuration.

ATR_20 used as the adaptive volatility breakout threshold. Signal is context-dependent: entry triggers when price volatility (ATR_20) exceeds previous close, suggesting a breakout. Exit uses SMA_20 as a trend confirmation filter. The original source text was corrupted/unreadable, so exit conditions were inferred as a reasonable trend-based reversal per Rule 8. ATR naturally adapts to changing price levels and volatility without fixed thresholds.

Condition 1: Wide-ranging bar detected as range > ATR_14 (above-average volatility bar). Condition 2: Rally and breakout above current day high represented as close > prev_high. Condition 3: Well-defined uptrend requirement operationalized as price above its 50-day MA and 50-day MA above 200-day MA (classic uptrend definition). Exit uses 20-day MA crossover as a reasonable trend reversal condition since author did not specify.

Signal uses context-dependent direction based on opening gap direction. Entry conditions fade (trade against) the opening direction when gap is ≤0.50% (0.005 in decimal scale). Exit condition triggers when a new high or low occurs relative to the opening price, interpreted as reversal signal. The constraint 'only if opening was not an extreme' is implicitly satisfied by the reversal logic—if opening was an extreme, the new high/low condition would not trigger a reversal trade. Gap size threshold set to 0.005 (0.50%) using returns indicator as proxy for opening gap magnitude.

Signal uses Bollinger Bands (upper/lower bands) for entry and exit. 'Upper band is broken' maps to close crossing above bb_upper; 'Lower band is crossed' maps to close crossing below bb_lower. System is always in market with reversal logic: exiting long when lower band crosses (triggering short entry) and vice versa. Volatility-dependent band width is an intrinsic property of Bollinger Bands calculation and does not require separate conditions.

Signal describes Bollinger Band penetration as either directional confirmation (trend-following) or mean reversion setup. Implemented as context-dependent with long entry on upper band penetration and short entry on lower band penetration. Exit conditions use middle band (SMA) as mean reversion target per standard Bollinger Band interpretation. The signal supports both directional and mean-reversion interpretations; this JSON implements the mean-reversion approach with optional directional bias based on entry choice. Parameters: 2 standard deviations (95% coverage) are implicit in bb_upper/bb_lower calculation.

Mapped 1.5 standard deviations cluster definition to Bollinger Bands (which use 2 standard deviations by default, but represent similar volatility-based clustering). Entry triggers when price returns into the band range (crosses back into cluster). Exit triggers when price breaks out of the cluster boundary. Context-dependent direction handles both long entries (price crosses above lower band) and short entries (price crosses below upper band) within the ranging regime.

Multi-timeframe trend-following strategy: sma_200 (weekly/daily longer-term trend proxy) combined with sma_20 (short-term entry timing). Entry occurs when both timeframes align directionally. Exit reverses when short-term trend breaks. Direction is context-dependent as signal applies to both long and short setups based on trend direction.

Signal uses longer-term MA (sma_50/sma_200 for trend identification) combined with shorter-period RSI (rsi_14) for entry timing. Direction is context-dependent based on trend direction. Exit conditions inferred as RSI reaching overbought/oversold extremes since author provided no explicit exit rules. Used rsi_14 as the shorter-period RSI option available; stochastic not used as RSI provides clearer signal. Trend confirmation via moving average crossover serves as trend line proxy.

Interpreted 'timing oscillator with period shorter than 5 days' as Stochastic with 5-day period (stoch_k_5). Overbought/oversold thresholds set at >80 and <20 respectively. Entry window of 3-5 days represented by hold_days_max of 5. Exit conditions based on reversal from extreme levels (approaching midpoint of 50). Direction marked CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as signal applies to both long and short entries based on oscillator extremes. Target regimes set to TRENDING as specified.

Interpreted 'previous day's highest high' as a 24-hour lookback period, approximated using Donchian Channel upper (dc_upper) which captures the highest high over a rolling window. The signal specifies a Buy Stop order on an hourly (60-minute) bar timeframe when price breaks above the previous day's high. Used dc_upper as a proxy for the highest high of the previous trading day. Exit condition set to a reasonable reversal (close below Donchian middle) since AUTHOR_SILENT was specified.

The 'Daily HiLo Activator' is a moving average of the highs. Since the exact period is marked AUTHOR_SILENT, sma_20 is used as a reasonable default for a daily trend-following moving average. Entry triggers when price (high) crosses above this MA (activator turning up). Exit triggers on reversal when price crosses below the MA (activator turning down). This implements the logic: enter on uptrend initiation after a downtrend, exit on trend reversal.

Signal uses volatility threshold filtering with two distinct entry regimes: low volatility (<0.25) and high volatility (>1.60). Mapped to hvol_20 (20-period historical volatility) as the closest available volatility indicator. Direction is context-dependent, so entry_long tests low volatility condition and entry_short tests high volatility condition. Exit conditions reverse the entry logic per standard reversal rule. No explicit stop loss or holding period specified by author.

CONTEXT_DEPENDENT direction mapped to both long and short entries with identical volatility filters. 40-day moving average signal interpreted as price crossing sma_40. Low volatility filter (0.25) applied as entry condition; high volatility filter (1.60) triggers exit. hvol_20 used as volatility metric (20-period historical volatility in decimal scale). Exit condition inverted from entry: exit when volatility rises above threshold, consistent with 'avoid entry when high volatility is present' logic.
Used stoch_k_5 for 5-day stochastic. Exit condition inferred as reversal (stochastic crosses below 50) per AUTHOR_SILENT rule.

Used ema_20 as the 20-day exponential (EMA is exponential moving average). Used prev_low for 'yesterday's low'. Used rsi_7 as closest available RSI period to the specified 10-day RSI (rsi_14 available but rsi_10 not in allowed list; rsi_7 is the closest match). Exit condition inferred as RSI crossing above 50 since AUTHOR_SILENT was specified.

Signal uses a 1:2 moving average ratio with unspecified periods. Implemented with sma_10 and sma_20 (1:2 ratio). Entry occurs on crossover in direction of trend; exit occurs on opposite crossover (reversal). No fixed stops used per source. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as the signal triggers both long and short positions based on MA crossover direction.

Signal uses 'exponential smoothing trendline' which maps to EMA. Selected ema_20 as a reasonable intermediate-term exponential moving average for trend following. The signal is context-dependent (both long and short entries), so both entry_long/exit_long and entry_short/exit_short are populated with mirror conditions. Exit conditions are reversal-based: long exits when price crosses below the trendline, short exits when price crosses above it.

N-day breakout strategy translated using Donchian Channel indicators (dc_upper and dc_lower) which represent the highest high and lowest low over a lookback period. The calculation_period_days parameter is embedded in the Donchian Channel calculation itself. Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT as the signal triggers both long entries (above highest high) and short entries (below lowest low). Exit conditions use reversal logic: long exits when price breaks below the lowest low, short exits when price breaks above the highest high.

Signal uses SMA crossover (short-term crosses long-term). Mapped to sma_10 (short-term, within 3-25 day range) and sma_20 (long-term, within 5-50 day range). Direction is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT: crosses_above triggers long entries and short exits; crosses_below triggers short entries and long exits. Exit conditions implemented as reversals per AUTHOR_SILENT rule. Target regime set to TRENDING per signal specification.

Signal uses context-dependent direction with MA crossover logic. Entry uses sma_5 (short-term) and sma_20 (long-term) as representative parameters within the specified ranges (short: 3-21, long: 10-80). Locked-limit day condition cannot be enforced at rule level. Always-in-market constraint is implicit in bidirectional entry/exit structure. Exit conditions are exact reversals of entry conditions per signal specification.

Signal specifies 'trend has just turned up' based on moving average. Interpreted as price crossing above a moving average (likely sma_20 as a standard trend indicator). Exit condition 'AUTHOR_SILENT' converted to reasonable reversal: price crossing below the same moving average. No explicit stop loss or hold period specified in original signal.

Interpreted 'innermost horizontal lines' as Bollinger Band upper/lower bands (slightly overbought/oversold thresholds). Center horizontal line mapped to bb_middle (mean). Entry conditions use band crossovers; exit conditions trigger when price returns to the mean line. Context-dependent direction supports both long (oversold buys) and short (overbought sells) entries based on which band is crossed.

Mapped 'faster moving average' to ema_10 and 'slower moving average' to ema_50 based on typical Kaufman-style multi-timeframe trend logic. 'Long-term moving average conflict' interpreted as ema_200 crossing conditions for neutral/exit signals. Entry conditions require price above both fast/slow MAs with fast MA below slow MA (bullish setup), reversed for shorts. Open interest measurement cannot be translated without futures-specific indicators; excluded from JSON. Signal is CONTEXT_DEPENDENT per source: buy on bullish MA alignment, sell on bearish alignment or long-term MA conflict.

Signal specifies RSI(8) but only rsi_7, rsi_14, rsi_21 are available. Used rsi_7 as closest match. Entry window of 5 days maximum is captured in hold_days_max. Direction is LONG (buy condition); SHORT exit omitted as signal is directional.

Signal describes a covered call strategy where entry occurs when stock is at or just below the 50 strike price. Entry conditions check that close is at or below strike (50) and reasonably close to it (>45 assumes typical strike proximity). Exit occurs when stock remains at/below strike at expiration, allowing premium retention. The 30-day hold approximates typical monthly expiration cycle. Strike price of 50 is hardcoded as specified in parameters. Position management (long stock + short call) is strategy-level context not directly translatable to price conditions.

Call spread entry requires price >= lower strike (50) and < higher strike (60) at entry to maximize profit potential. Exit condition triggers when price reaches or exceeds higher strike (60), realizing the maximum 10-point profit. Stop loss set at 2% to manage downside risk if underlying moves below lower strike. Hold period set to 30 days to approximate October expiration window. The 'expectation of relatively unchanged price' is implicit in the RANGING regime filter and the entry/exit price boundaries.

Strangle strategy: Entry requires underlying between strike prices (45-50 OTM range) with elevated volatility. Exit triggers at profitable breakout levels (>54 or <41) or total loss if expiration occurs between strikes. Strike prices (call=50, put=45) hardcoded into conditions. Max hold set to 31 days (January expiration). Stop loss set to 100% reflecting max loss on strangle premium. hvol_20 used as proxy for volatility regime confirmation.

Strangle write strategy: Entry when price is between lower strike (60) and upper strike (70). Exit condition uses upside breakeven (77 = 70 call strike + 7 points premium). The signal specifies holding to expiration where profit occurs if price remains between strikes. Actual strike prices (70 call, 60 put) and premiums (4 and 3 = 7 total) are author-silent, so conditions use the concrete example values from the source text. Exit at upside breakeven represents a defined risk management point; downside exit would trigger at 53 breakeven (60 - 7) but configured as primary exit for simplicity.

Iron condor / credit spread entry occurs when stock is between the two strikes (60 put strike and 70 call strike). Exit triggered if stock rallies above the call strike (70), threatening unlimited loss on the short call. Maximum hold is set to typical monthly options expiration (~30 days). Stop loss set to 1% as conservative risk management on the 1-point net credit received. The strategy profits if stock stays between 60-70 at expiration (both options expire worthless).

Put bear spread (debit spread) strategy. Entry: initiate spread when price is between the two strikes (50-60). Exit: maximum profit realized when price closes below lower strike (50) at expiration. Strike prices (60 long, 50 short) and January expiration are position parameters rather than dynamic indicator conditions. This is a vertical spread strategy with defined risk/reward and expiration-based exit.

Put ratio spread (1 long put at 50 strike, 2 short puts at 45 strike). Entry when price enters profitable range (40-50). Exit triggered if price moves above upper strike (50). Maximum profit occurs at short strike (45). Stop loss would be triggered if price falls below long strike (40), but not explicitly modeled here as ratio spreads have defined max loss. Signal uses options pricing logic not directly expressible via technical indicators alone, but price-level conditions capture the key risk/reward zones.

Signal is an options expiration strategy. Entry condition checks if underlying is above 60 at expiration. Exit triggers when close is above 60 (at expiration), liquidating the call bull spread for maximum 10-point profit. The 10-point maximum profit is the strike difference and represents the upper bound of the spread's P&L, not a stop-loss level. Hold is set to 1 day to represent expiration evaluation. Actual position sizing and strike parameters would be managed separately in the options contract specification.

Event-driven merger arbitrage signal (McMillan DRY 198). Entry when XYZ closes below the 45 threshold ('hook' level in merger). Exit when price rallies back above 45, representing the damaging loss scenario described. The ratio hedge (0.556 XYZ short per LMN long) and LMN position management are positional accounting details outside the scope of price-based entry/exit rules. Signal context indicates pre-merger conditions with CONTEXT_DEPENDENT direction resolved to SHORT based on explicit short position instruction.

Used india_vix as proxy for implied volatility. Donchian channels (dc_upper, dc_lower, dc_middle) represent the long-term established trading range. Entry signals trigger at range extremes (near lows for long, near highs for short). Exit conditions target the midpoint of range or reversal, consistent with mean-reversion behavior. The signal is inherently context-dependent (buy lows/sell highs), so entry conditions are split between long and short directions.

Signal requires implied volatility percentile tracking. Used india_vix as proxy for volatility extreme (>=95 approximates 100th percentile extreme). Entry triggers on volatility spike. Exit condition implemented as reversal: when current volatility drops below previous close value, indicating directional reversal. This interpretation aligns with the author's intent for options sellers to avoid entering during volatility spikes and to exit when volatility reverses downward.

Signal applies to SHORT options selling positions only (direction=SHORT). VIX mapped to india_vix (closest available volatility indicator). EWMA(60, 0.95) approximated using ema_20 as the closest available exponential moving average; note that the specified decay factor and period cannot be exactly matched with available indicators, but ema_20 provides similar regime filtering behavior. Entry condition: sell options when VIX below its moving average. Exit condition: cover when VIX crosses above moving average. Target regimes indicate preference for low-volatility environments where short options strategies perform better.

This signal emphasizes regime filtering and setup pattern matching rather than specific entry/exit prices. Translated as: enter when trend strength is confirmed (ADX > 25 indicating strong trending environment), exit when trend weakens (ADX < 20). The core rule is a meta-strategy principle—wait for favorable regime match. ADX_14 serves as a proxy for trending regime detection. The actual setup pattern criteria would need to be defined separately by the trader implementing this rule.
